title: Bounded context ownership
question: In a microservices architecture, which rule defines a bounded context?
answer: A service owns its data exclusively — no other service can touch its database directly
options: A service owns its data exclusively — no other service can touch its database directly
A service shares its database with at most one other service
A service is limited to a single HTTP endpoint
A service must be deployed on its own server

title: Service map arrows
question: On a GameHub service map, what does a dashed arrow between two services represent?
options: Asynchronous communication (event/message queue)
Synchronous REST call
A shared database connection
An optional/unused link

title: Conway's Law
question: Conway's Law states that a system's architecture tends to mirror what?
answer: The communication structure of the organisation that built it
options: The communication structure of the organisation that built it
The programming language chosen for each service
The number of developers on the team
The deployment frequency of the system

title: notification-service language
question: Why does notification-service use Node.js while the rest of GameHub uses Python?
answer: Microservices allow each service to use the best technology for its job — language choice is a per-service decision
options: Microservices allow each service to use the best technology for its job — language choice is a per-service decision
Python cannot send emails or push notifications
Node.js services are always faster than Python services
The course required at least one non-Python service for grading purposes

title: FastAPI layering — repository
question: In the GameHub layered architecture, what is the sole responsibility of the repository layer?
answer: Raw database queries — no business logic
options: Raw database queries — no business logic
HTTP routing and request validation
Business logic and orchestration
Pydantic schema definitions

title: FastAPI layering — schemas
question: Which layer holds the Pydantic DTOs that define what the API sends and receives?
options: schemas
models
repository
routes

title: Alembic purpose
question: What does running `alembic upgrade head` do in a GameHub service?
answer: Applies all pending database migrations up to the latest version
options: Applies all pending database migrations up to the latest version
Starts the FastAPI development server
Runs the pytest test suite
Generates a new SQLAlchemy model file

title: ilike operator
question: Which SQLAlchemy operator is used for the `/v1/games/search?q=<term>` endpoint to perform a case-insensitive partial match?
options: ilike
like
contains
match

title: Gateway port
question: On which port does the GameHub gateway listen, and what is its role?
answer: Port 8000 — single entry point that forwards all client requests to the correct downstream service
options: Port 8000 — single entry point that forwards all client requests to the correct downstream service
Port 8001 — user-service endpoint
Port 8080 — load balancer for all services
Port 8000 — runs business logic for all services

title: Gateway unknown route
question: A request arrives at the gateway for `/v1/unknown`. What HTTP status should it return?
options: 200 with an empty body

title: Gateway downstream unreachable
question: A request is forwarded to user-service but the service is not running. What HTTP status should the gateway return to the client?
options: 503 Service Unavailable
404 Not Found
500 Internal Server Error
200 with a null body

title: Validation vs enrichment
question: When activity-service creates an activity, it calls user-service to validate the user and game-service to enrich the response. What happens when game-service is unreachable?
answer: The activity is saved anyway and the response includes "game": null
options: The activity is saved anyway and the response includes "game": null
The request fails with a 503 error
The activity is saved but no response is returned to the client
The request is retried up to 3 times before failing

title: Retry library
question: Which Python library is used in Module 3 to add retry logic to the user-service validation call?
options: tenacity
retry
backoff
httpx

title: Cascading failure risk
question: Three services are chained synchronously: gateway → activity-service → user-service. If user-service takes 3 seconds to respond, what is the effect on the client?
answer: The client waits at least 3 seconds — latency compounds across the entire chain
options: The client waits at least 3 seconds — latency compounds across the entire chain
The gateway times out immediately and returns 200
Only activity-service is affected; the client sees no delay
The chain runs in parallel so the client waits only 1 second

title: Why async for activity→logging
question: Why is it better to publish activity events to logging-service via a message queue (async) rather than a direct REST call (sync)?
answer: A slow or down logging-service would block and fail every activity creation if the call were synchronous
options: A slow or down logging-service would block and fail every activity creation if the call were synchronous
REST calls cannot carry the required payload size
Async is always faster than sync regardless of context
The gateway cannot route to logging-service
